summary: I am originally from Lackawanna, New York, a town right on the shores of Lake Erie. In the early 1900s, Lackawanna was home to the largest steel plant in the world, which played a big role in my family's life. My father and grandparents settled there, living just across from the steel plant. It was a close-knit community with many Italian immigrants like us.

Giovanni Andreozzi was my grandfather on my father's side. He was born in Falvaterra, Lazio, just south of Rome, and came to the United States in 1909. He settled in Lackawanna, New York, married my grandmother Vincenza Limongiello in 1915, and they had three children. Sadly, he died suddenly in 1918 from the Spanish flu at Columbus Hospital in Buffalo.

Falvaterra is the small town in Lazio, Italy, where my grandfather Giovanni Andreozzi was born. It's located just south of Rome, and it was from there that he emigrated to the United States in 1909. This place marks the beginning of our family's journey to America and the life they built in New York.

Lazio is the region in Italy where my grandfather Giovanni was born, specifically in the town of Falvaterra. It's just south of Rome, and it's part of central Italy. This region is important to my family's roots before they immigrated to the United States in the early 20th century.

Rome is the capital city near where my grandfather Giovanni was born, in the Lazio region. While he came from a smaller town south of Rome, the proximity to such a historic city is part of our family's Italian heritage. It gives context to the area he left behind when he came to America in 1909.

Buffalo, New York was the first place my grandfather Giovanni arrived at when he came to America in 1909. My grandmother Vincenza was born in Buffalo, in a tough waterfront neighborhood called the Hooks. Buffalo was a hub for many Italian immigrants like my family before settling in nearby Lackawanna.

Lake Erie is the large lake right next to Lackawanna, New York, where my family lived. The steel plant was built on its shores around 1900, and it shaped the town's economy and my family's livelihood. Growing up near Lake Erie was part of our daily life in Lackawanna.

The steel plant in Lackawanna was the largest in the world when it was built around 1900. It was right across from where my grandparents lived and provided jobs that attracted many immigrants, including my family. The plant was central to the community and shaped the economic opportunities for us.

My grandmother, Vincenza Limongiello, was born in the Hooks, a tough waterfront tenement in Buffalo, New York. She changed her name to Jenny Limoncello to make it easier for Americans to pronounce. She married my grandfather Giovanni in late 1915, and they raised three children in Lackawanna before her tragic losses in 1918.

The Hooks was a tough waterfront neighborhood in Buffalo, New York, where my grandmother Vincenza Limongiello was born. It was a working-class area with many Italian immigrants like my family. Growing up there shaped her early life before she married and moved to Lackawanna.

Filippo Andreozzi, also known as Phillip, was my father and the first child of Giovanni and Jenny. He was named after his uncle and grew up in Lackawanna, New York. He was healthy as a child but experienced the loss of his siblings and father during the Spanish flu pandemic.

Maria Giuseppe was my aunt, the second child of my grandparents Giovanni and Jenny. She was born in 1918 but tragically died within three months in August of that year. Her death was one of the early family losses during a difficult time marked by the Spanish flu.

Columbus Hospital in Buffalo was known as the Italian Hospital and is where my grandfather Giovanni was taken when he fell ill with the Spanish flu in 1918. He died there within a week, marking a tragic moment for our family. The hospital served many Italian immigrants like my family during that time.

The Spanish flu was a devastating pandemic in 1918 that affected my family deeply. My grandfather Giovanni died from it at Columbus Hospital, and my aunt Maria Giuseppe also passed away that year. The flu left a lasting impact on our family and many others in the Lackawanna community.
cues: death of Giovanni and Maria Giuseppe

summary: Tony Andreozzi was my father's stepfather, who married my grandmother Jenny a year after Giovanni died. My father grew up thinking Tony was his real father until someone in their Italian neighborhood told him otherwise. Tony became part of our family story, even though he was not my father's biological dad.

Calabria is the region in Southern Italy where my mother was born, near Cosenza. It's the 'toe' of the Italian boot and home to many immigrants who came to America. My mother's family had strong ties there before moving to the United States in the 1920s.

Cosenza is a province in Calabria, Southern Italy, near where my mother was born in the town of Marano Principato. It represents the rural Italian origins of my maternal family before they immigrated to the United States. This area shaped much of my mother's early life and heritage.

Marano Principato is the small town in Calabria where my mother was born on January 6th, 1918. It's a quiet place near Cosenza and was home to my mother's family before they moved to America. Her early years there are part of our family's Italian story.

Filiberto Covelli was my maternal grandfather who moved back and forth between Calabria and the United States starting in 1899. He initially went to Chicago, then to Kenosha, Wisconsin, where many from his town settled. Eventually, he moved to Lackawanna for better-paying jobs and sent for his family to join him in 1927.

Chicago was the first American city my maternal grandfather Filiberto Covelli arrived in after leaving Calabria. It was a common destination for many Calabresi immigrants in the late 19th and early 20th centuries. From there, he moved on to Kenosha, Wisconsin, continuing his journey in America.

Kenosha, Wisconsin was where my grandfather Filiberto Covelli settled after Chicago, joining a community of people from his hometown. He worked there before moving to Lackawanna, New York, seeking better-paying jobs. Kenosha was an important stop in my family's migration story in America.

Rosina Ruffolo-Covelli was my mother's real mother and a ghost in our family history because she died suddenly in 1925 when my mother was still a child. She lived in Calabria and left a lasting impression on my mother, who remembered her through stories. Rosina's early death deeply affected the family, and we often wondered how life might have been if she had survived.

Holy Cross Cemetery in Lackawanna is where my grandfather Giovanni and other family members who died from the Spanish flu were buried. Their graves were unmarked for many years until my siblings and I raised money to place markers. This cemetery holds a significant place in our family's history and remembrance.
